metadata.subject: Social Studies
metadata.description: B.C. First Peoples 12 examines the histories, cultures, governance, and contemporary experiences of British Columbia's First Peoples. With a strong emphasis on land-based identity, oral tradition, and self-determination, the course traces the impacts of contact and colonialism while centring Indigenous voices, legal precedents, and resistance movements. Students engage with Indigenous ways of knowing and build tools for understanding reconciliation.
metadata.general_description: An in-depth study of BC First Peoples from pre-contact to present, examining land relationships, oral tradition, colonial history, cultural expression, governance, and the ongoing path toward self-determination and reconciliation.
metadata.course_notes: This course centres Indigenous perspectives, oral traditions, and community voices. Engagement with local Elders and knowledge keepers is strongly encouraged.

big_ideas.statement: The identities, worldviews, and languages of B.C. First Peoples are renewed, sustained, and transformed through their connection to the land.
big_ideas.elaboration: Land is not simply territory — for BC First Peoples, it is the foundation of identity, spiritual practice, governance, and cultural continuity.

big_ideas.statement: The impact of contact and colonialism continues to affect the political, social, and economic lives of B.C. First Peoples.
big_ideas.elaboration: From the Indian Act to residential schools, colonial policies created systemic inequities whose effects are felt across generations and are still being addressed today.

big_ideas.statement: Cultural expressions convey the richness, diversity, and resiliency of B.C. First Peoples.
big_ideas.elaboration: Through art, ceremony, language, and storytelling, BC First Peoples have maintained and revitalized their cultures even in the face of suppression and forced assimilation.

big_ideas.statement: Through self-governance, leadership, and self-determination, B.C. First Peoples challenge and resist Canada's ongoing colonialism.
big_ideas.elaboration: Legal victories, political organizing, land claims, and cultural revitalization all represent active resistance to colonial structures and the assertion of Indigenous sovereignty.

categories.name: Use Social Studies inquiry processes and skills
categories.raw: Use Social Studies inquiry processes and skills to ask questions; gather, interpret, and analyze ideas; and communicate findings and decisions.

competencies.raw: Draw conclusions, assess positions, demonstrate leadership, identify problems, evaluate data, interpret maps/graphs/tables, cite sources, construct visual representations.
i_can_statements: I can form my own questions about BC First Peoples history and follow them through research.
I can evaluate a wide range of sources, including oral tradition and Indigenous scholarship.
I can present my findings in a way that accurately and respectfully represents Indigenous perspectives.
I can lead or contribute meaningfully to collaborative inquiry.

categories.name: Significance
categories.raw: Assess the significance of people, events, places, issues, or developments in the past and present.

competencies.raw: Assess the significance of people, events, places, issues, or developments in the past and present. Identify what creators of accounts, narratives, or maps have determined to be significant.
i_can_statements: I can explain why a court case, activist, or cultural event is significant to BC First Peoples.
I can analyze what a historical map or colonial document chose to emphasize and what it left out.
I can compare how the same event is viewed as significant from Indigenous and settler perspectives.

categories.name: Evidence (including Indigenous ways of knowing)
categories.raw: Using appropriate protocols, interpret a variety of sources, including local stories or oral traditions, and Indigenous ways of knowing (holistic, experiential, reflective, and relational experiences, and memory) to contextualize different events in the past and present.

competencies.raw: Interpret oral traditions and Indigenous ways of knowing with appropriate protocols to contextualize events.
i_can_statements: I can explain what oral tradition is and why it is a valid form of historical evidence.
I can use appropriate protocols when engaging with Elders or knowledge keepers.
I can analyze how the Delgamuukw v. BC (1997) ruling recognized oral history as legal evidence.
I can compare Indigenous relational and holistic ways of knowing with Western analytical approaches.

categories.name: Continuity and Change
categories.raw: Characterize different time periods in history, including examples of progress and decline, and identify key turning points that marked periods of change. Assess the long- and short-term causes and consequences, and the intended and unintended consequences, of an action, event, decision, or development.

competencies.raw: Characterize different time periods in BC First Peoples history and identify key turning points.
i_can_statements: I can identify a turning point in BC First Peoples history and explain what changed.
I can trace how the Indian Act has changed over time and evaluate what impact those changes had.
I can explain what 'spiralling' time means in an Indigenous worldview versus the Western linear view of time.

categories.name: Cause and Consequence
categories.raw: Assess the long- and short-term causes and consequences, and the intended and unintended consequences, of an action, event, decision, or development.

competencies.raw: Assess the long- and short-term causes and consequences of colonial policies and Indigenous responses.
i_can_statements: I can trace the long-term consequences of the residential school system on individuals, families, and communities.
I can identify both the intended and unintended consequences of Canadian government policies toward First Peoples.
I can explain the concept of intergenerational trauma and how it connects to colonial history.

categories.name: Connectedness and Sense of Place
categories.raw: Assess the connectedness or the reciprocal relationship between people and place.

competencies.raw: Assess the connectedness and reciprocal relationship between people and place.
i_can_statements: I can explain how a specific First Nation's culture is connected to their traditional territory.
I can analyze how the loss of land impacted the cultural, spiritual, and economic life of Indigenous communities.
I can describe the concept of stewardship and explain how it differs from Western concepts of land ownership.

categories.name: Perspective
categories.raw: Explain different perspectives on past and present people, places, issues, or events, and distinguish between worldviews of today and the past. Explain and infer perspectives and sense of place, and compare varying perspectives on land and place.

competencies.raw: Explain different perspectives on past and present issues and compare varying perspectives on land and place.
i_can_statements: I can explain how Indigenous and settler worldviews differ in their understanding of land, time, and community.
I can take on the perspective of a First Nation community in analyzing a land claim or treaty negotiation.
I can analyze media representations of First Peoples and identify stereotypes or biases.

categories.name: Ethical Judgment
categories.raw: Make reasoned ethical judgments about actions in the past and present, and assess appropriate ways to remember, reconcile, or respond.

competencies.raw: Make reasoned ethical judgments and assess appropriate ways to remember, reconcile, or respond.
i_can_statements: I can evaluate the adequacy of government apologies and redress for historical injustices.
I can discuss what reconciliation requires — from individuals, from governments, and from society.
I can make an ethical argument about the responsibility of non-Indigenous Canadians in the process of reconciliation.

areas.name: Traditional Territories and Land Relationships
areas.raw: Traditional territories of local First Nations and overlapping boundaries; political boundaries versus traditional territories; land's influence on First Peoples worldview, stewardship, and cultural practices; cultural and linguistic diversity among B.C. First Peoples.
topics: Traditional territories of BC First Nations and their overlapping nature
Distinction between political/colonial boundaries and traditional territories
Land as the foundation of identity, spirituality, and governance
Stewardship versus ownership: Indigenous environmental relationships
Cultural and linguistic diversity: the many distinct nations and languages of BC

areas.name: Oral Tradition
areas.raw: Elders as knowledge keepers; oral tradition as valid legal evidence (referencing Delgamuukw v. B.C., 1997); stories, songs, music, dance as narrative forms; Indigenous concepts of time (spiralling versus linear).
topics: The role of Elders as knowledge keepers and community leaders
Oral tradition: its forms, purposes, and protocols
Delgamuukw v. BC (1997): oral history recognized as legal evidence
Songs, dances, and visual art as carriers of history and law
Spiralling vs. linear time: an Indigenous conceptual framework

areas.name: Historical Exchanges
areas.raw: Trade networks and routes; settlement and migration patterns; maritime and land fur trade; exchange of goods, technology, economy, knowledge; industries (gold rush, whaling).
topics: Pre-contact trade networks among BC First Nations
The maritime fur trade and its impacts
The land-based fur trade: the HBC and its effects on Indigenous communities
The gold rush and its disruption of First Peoples territories
Whaling industries and their impact on coastal nations
Exchange of goods, technologies, and knowledge with Europeans

areas.name: Government Policies and Colonialism
areas.raw: Indian Act and amendments; enfranchisement; White Paper, Red Paper (Alberta), Brown Paper (B.C.); residential schools; federal apology; TRC; treaties regarding fishing and hunting rights; Sixties Scoop; foster care system; Meech Lake, Charlottetown Accords, Canadian Charter; UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples.
topics: The Indian Act (1876): provisions, intent, and impacts
Amendments to the Indian Act over time
Enfranchisement: the forced loss of Indian status
The White Paper (1969) and the Red/Brown Paper responses
Residential schools: purpose, operation, abuse, and intergenerational trauma
The federal apology (2008) and its limitations
The Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC) and the 94 Calls to Action
Treaty rights: fishing, hunting, and their legal protection
The Sixties Scoop and the ongoing over-representation of Indigenous children in foster care
The Meech Lake and Charlottetown Accords and Indigenous rights
The Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms and its impact on Indigenous peoples
The UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP)

areas.name: Resistance and Activism
areas.raw: Political actions (Union of British Columbia Indian Chiefs, Métis Nation British Columbia); Tsilhqot'in War; Gustafsen Lake standoff; Idle No More; judicial cases (Calder 1973; Guerin 1984; Sparrow 1990; Van der Peet 1996); Cindy Blackstock and Canadian Human Rights Tribunal; ecological justice protests.
topics: The Union of British Columbia Indian Chiefs (UBCIC) and political organizing
The Métis Nation British Columbia and Métis rights
The Tsilhqot'in War (1864–65) and its legacy
The Gustafsen Lake standoff (1995)
Idle No More (2012): origins, significance, and impact
Landmark court cases: Calder (1973), Guerin (1984), Sparrow (1990), Van der Peet (1996)
Cindy Blackstock and the Canadian Human Rights Tribunal ruling on child welfare
Ecological justice protests: pipelines, LNG, logging, fracking, and hydroelectricity

areas.name: Media and Cultural Representation
areas.raw: Portrayal of First Peoples in media; repatriation and ownership of cultural objects; copyright, patent rights, intellectual property, and appropriation ethics.
topics: Stereotypes and misrepresentation of Indigenous peoples in mainstream media
Cultural appropriation: what it is and why it matters
Repatriation: returning cultural objects and human remains
Intellectual property rights and Indigenous knowledge
Indigenous-led media and cultural production
Social media and Indigenous resistance and identity

areas.name: Governance Systems
areas.raw: Traditional governance structures; band system; land claims and self-governance.
topics: Traditional governance structures: chiefs, councils, clan systems
The imposed band council system under the Indian Act
Self-governance agreements and their evolution
Modern treaty negotiations in BC
First Nations in BC with self-governance agreements
Urban Indigenous governance and community organizations

areas.name: Contemporary Challenges
areas.raw: Missing and murdered Indigenous women; stereotypes and institutionalized racism; intergenerational trauma; judicial and correctional system issues; child welfare system; reserve conditions (water, housing, education).
topics: Missing and Murdered Indigenous Women and Girls (MMIWG): the national inquiry and its findings
Stereotypes and institutionalized racism in Canadian institutions
Intergenerational trauma: definition, impact, and healing
Over-representation of Indigenous peoples in the criminal justice system
The child welfare system and Indigenous children
Conditions on reserves: access to clean water, housing shortages, education inequities
